title: Longshoremen Push Back on Galveston Port Deal as Mediation Begins
body:
Mediation talks between the Port of Galveston, the International Longshoremen's Association, and automation contractor Stelmark Freight got underway Monday with little visible sign of progress, participants said.

The ILA's Gulf Coast regional director, Clyde Okafor, arrived at the session with a 47-page counter-proposal that would require Stelmark to cap automated crane deployments at two units in the first phase and commit to a workforce transition fund of at least $18 million. Stelmark's chief operating officer, Miriam Solano, confirmed the company received the proposal but said her team needed time to review it.

The port authority chair, Bernard Tran, said he hoped for a resolution within the 60-day window to avoid jeopardizing the federal grant. "The DOT has been patient with us, but the clock is real," Tran told reporters outside the mediation venue.

Washington-based lobbyist Renata Solis, who represents a coalition of port automation suppliers, attended the session as an observer. Solis has been active on Capitol Hill in recent months, advocating for DOT grant rules that explicitly protect automation investments from labor agreement overrides.

The ILA has also filed a formal complaint with the National Labor Relations Board, alleging the port authority failed to bargain in good faith before awarding the Stelmark contract.
